questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B with 86M parameters. Note: ViT-L (307M) appears in the paper as a comparison baseline, not as a DeiT model variant.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed in the evidence is ViT-L, with 307M parameters. If you mean the largest DeiT variant in this paper, DeiT-B★ is 87M parameters.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design yields linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice highlighted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper uses DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer setup based on self-distillation without labels. A student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening to prevent collapse; the paper evaluates this on ViTs (especially small-patch variants) and also on convnets like ResNet-50.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ImageNet top-1 accuracies are highly inconsistent across papers, ranging from 1.3 to 91.0 (spread 89.7, stddev 8.69). The corpus also contains multiple conflicting SOTA claims, with different papers reporting top values such as 91.0 (CoCa finetuned), 90.9 (PaLI-17B), 90.6 (CoCa frozen), and 90.0 (MLP-Mixer), so there is no single consistent top-1 value.

questions.system_answer: The corpus evidence does not provide any CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values, so the minimum and maximum cannot be determined from the supplied data.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model: one paper reports InternImage-H at 62.9 as the top claim, while another claims ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is also listed at 59.9. The values vary widely overall (26.6 to 62.9), so there are conflicting SOTA claims rather than consensus.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The ADE20K mIoU values in the corpus vary widely, from 26.6 to 62.9 (a spread of 36.3, stddev 7.29), so they are not consistent. The highest SOTA claim is InternImage-H at 62.9, while other papers report lower SOTA values such as ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9, indicating differing reported results rather than a single consistent value.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: The corpus does not show a direct disagreement about necessity. The evidence consistently says positional information is commonly used in vision transformers because self-attention is permutation-equivariant: one paper states, "positional encodings are commonly employed to incorporate the order of sequences" and that "Vision transformers follow the same fashion to imbue the network with positional information." Another paper frames its contribution as "provide the position information in vision transformers," which supports the view that position embeddings are important, but the evidence here does not include any paper explicitly claiming they are unnecessary.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy increased from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021, then rose slightly to 91.0 in 2022. After that, it declined year over year, dropping to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up from 2020 to 2022, then down through 2024.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
